上周，亚利桑那州＆＃39;众议院的代表批准了立法，以禁止平台所有者喜欢苹果和谷歌将应用程序制造商锁定到自己的支付系统中。该法案仅狭隘地通过，必须得到亚利桑那州参议院和哥夫斯·杜格特的批准，然后才能成为法律。但是，无论票据＆＃39;终极命运，投票是公众对硅谷的公共态度戏剧性转变的最新迹象。
 在互联网时代的前二十年来，政治家应该与沉重的规则和法规融为一体的广泛共识。像Apple，Amazon，Google等公司被广泛钦佩。 2007年，双方的总统候选人致敬与谷歌联系起来。 2015年，JEB布什，TED Cruz和其他共和国希望互相绊倒，以定位自己是最优雅的候选人。
  科技公司＆＃39;威望并支持他们的政治权力。那些拟议的关于技术公司的规定 - 或者在某些情况下只想将他们视为与其他公司相同的规则 - 经常被视为无触及的反动度和进展的敌人。但是所有改变的。像YouTube，iPhone和乘车的产品一样的产品已成为现代生活的平等部分。他们背后的公司被广泛认为是根深蒂固的现任者。这样一来，市民和他们选出的代表越来越像对待其他大的，有实力的公司。政治家通过栏杆对抗它们得分。提案有时会降低他们。
 未来几年将对Apple，Google，Facebook和Uber等公司尤其具有挑战性，因为它们是全球公司。他们不仅必须担心美国联邦政府的反托拉斯诉讼，他们也面临着美国各国和世界各国政府的沉重审查。对于科技巨头，有一个司法管辖区的政策实验可能成为一个先前的危险，可以成为世界各地的先例。
   返回2016年，德克萨斯州奥斯汀市希望优步和Lyft到指纹司机 - 这一规则已经适用于该市的普通出租车司机。这个想法是批准的56％的城市＆＃39;选民。优步和Lyft通过核对奥斯汀运营来回应，直到城市改变了规则。
 奥斯汀官员认为，他们只是要求优步和Lyft与其他出租车提供者相同的规则。但是，许多公众成员，特别是服务的客户，买入了一个不同的叙述：奥斯汀官员脱离了现代技术。他们背后有一部分公众，给予了优化的电力电源Uber和Lyft最终习惯于获得州立法机构覆盖奥斯汀＆＃39;规则。
 这不是一个胜利的胜利。它＆＃39;一本公司在初期一遍又一遍地使用的剧本。优步和Lyft会经常俯冲到一个新的大都市区，而不是担心当地出租车规定。在风险投资中花费数百万，他们尝试尽快建立客户群。城市官员担心这些客户的反障，他们希望被视为站在进步方面。所以他们几乎总是有所缓解。 
近年来，这种策略变得效益较低。部分地点＆＃39;因为活动家为这些公司产生了更多负面的压力机。一旦风险投资补贴的喷气机干涸，驾驶为优步或Lyft被证明是一个低薪和岌岌可危的演出。批评者还强调了优步和Lyft车辆中发生的性侵犯问题 - 尽管它不明确，如果优步和Lyft在这里有更大的问题，而不是传统的出租车。
 但也许最重要的转变是优步和Lyft不再被视为弱者。在2010年初，这些公司＆＃39;生存似乎似乎是一个开放的问题。客户担心现任出租车公司将使用过时的法规来扼杀摇篮。因此，当优步和Lyft认为某个特定的规定可能迫使他们留下一个城市或国家时，客户相信他们，有些人在那里宣传他们所选的官员退缩。
  今天这只是一个严重关注的人。优步和Lyft是数十个城市运营的多亿美元公司。许多司法管辖区已通过立法明确允许这种类型的服务运行。因此，这些公司发现越来越困难，努力监管他们的商业惯例。 2019年，加利福尼亚通过了立法要求优步和Lyft作为员工将其司机视为司机。因为他们已经完成了过去，如果选民没有推翻法律，那就曾经在过去做过的那段，优步和莱特威胁要关闭他们的服务。选民最终批准了由Uber和Lyft赞助的选票。但是优步和Lyft被迫做出一些战术让步;选民批准的措施提供了演出经济的司机司机一些增加的保护。
 在美国以外的优先权来说，事情更加糟糕。上个月，英国最高法院统治了优步可以像独立承包商那样对待其司机。法国和西班牙的法院已达到类似的结论 - 尽管优步似乎仍然抵制了法国的员工模型。
 去年，马萨诸塞州提出了对Uber和Lyft的诉讼，争论他们的司机是国家法律的雇员。安大略省的一个类动作诉讼是类似的索赔。
 在每个司法管辖区中，一个关键的优步论证是强制执行传统的劳动法将破坏Uber＆＃39; S按需商业模式。这种论点很难评估，因为优步已经成功地击败了这些努力。但优雅的可能需要改变它在未来几年内的英国，法国，西班牙和可能其他司法管辖区的业务。
 如果优步设法提出一个可行的模式，将司机视为员工，它将面临在其他城市采用相同模型的压力。虽然当然也是可能的：也许是一个基于员工的模型的交换机将使优步和第39; S服务在法国和西班牙等地方大幅越来越糟糕，加强优步和＃39;在未来的战斗中。 
一些新闻组织长期以来归咎于谷歌和Facebook，用于消耗前往报纸和其他新闻媒体的广告收入。 2014年，西班牙政府通过了立法，要求谷歌支付才能链接到新闻文章。
 谷歌以与奥斯汀和第39位的立法作出反应的方式对此立法相同，与奥斯汀响应：通过关闭西班牙语版谷歌新闻。向西班牙新闻网站的流量陷入了困扰，损害了他们的广告收入。西班牙政府最终被迫投产。像奥斯汀的优步和Lyft一样，谷歌描绘了西班牙官员，就像互联网如何工作的触摸和无知。
     谷歌与西班牙法律的焦炭 - 地球反应给了公司几年＆＃39;受到这些＆＃34;链接税和＃34;提案。但在2019年，他们用复仇了。欧洲议会通过了一个版权大修，使新闻机构给予复制＆＃34的人的新权利。片段＆＃34;新闻故事。法国的官员是第一个实施新规则的国家之一，警告谷歌将允许它允许避免使用新闻网站来避免薪水。所以谷歌开始付钱。当澳大利亚开始辩论自己的＆＃34;链接税和＃34;针对谷歌和Facebook的提案，公司威胁要从澳大利亚市场拉出他们的产品。 2月，Facebook禁止澳大利亚用户从Facebook发布新闻报道，就像谷歌在西班牙七年之前完成的那样。但这一次结果相当不同。澳大利亚官员发誓不要让Facebook恐吓他们。 Facebook确实涉及澳大利亚政府的一些让步，但最终通过立法通过了有效地要求谷歌和Facebook来支付链接到新闻报道。
 当然，其他国家的领导人一直密切监测澳大利亚和法国摊牌。现在，这些国家展示了如何盯着谷歌和Facebook，我们可以预期其他国家遵循他们的榜样。据报道，加拿大政府致力于类似于澳大利亚的立法。我们可以预期很多欧盟成员国遵守法国模型，因为他们实施了欧盟版权指令。
   在App Store政策上的亚利桑那州的斗争与新闻和乘车的战斗以及一些差异有一些相似之处。一个重要的区别是苹果和谷歌可以＆＃39; t可靠地威胁要将他们的智能手机平台从亚利桑那州推出。
 另一方面，这是另一种案例，科技公司在许多不同的前端作出类似的立法提案。例如，苹果击败了北达科他州的立法，要求公司允许iOS应用程序使用第三方支付机制。在付款战斗中，Apple＆＃39;在支付战斗中的主要主角，还向欧洲联盟，英国和澳大利亚的竞争监管机构提供了正式的投诉。
 它不可能预测任何这些特定的战斗将如何发挥作用，但似乎史诗似乎将在这些场地中的至少一个。这可能强迫Apple将其App Store开放到世界某个地方的第三方付款。一旦他们所做的一旦完成了，在其他国家的官员将更容易按Apple为自己的消费者和开发人员提供相同的选择。 
与此同时，大科技公司正面临更广泛的调查 - 在某些情况下，诉讼 - 来自一些政府。美国政府与52个国家和地区起诉谷歌在去年年底，在搜索和广告市场中的反竞争行为。联邦贸易委员会和47个国家的另一个诉讼旨在分解Facebook。欧洲竞争当局正在调查几个科技巨头。澳大利亚官员表示，他们看到新闻谈判规则只是规范科技巨头的第一步。简而言之，硅谷公司似乎越来越被迫在世界各国政府的政策偏好下指关节。集中科技公司权力的批评者可能会将其视为积极的发展。但它也可能有一个很大的缺点：使公司在国界跨国公司运营更加困难。
 关于互联网的伟大事物之一是一个国家在一个国家的软件公司迅速扩展的软件公司，并为世界各地的客户提供产品。但是，如果国家，各国和其他司法管辖区采用越来越多的灌木丛，管理在线服务如何工作，在多个司法管辖区内将更加努力地提供单一产品。像苹果和谷歌这样的大公司将弄清楚。小公司可能会挣扎。
 另一方面，在过去十年里，主要技术哈维登哈登＆＃39; T面临着很多严肃的新竞争对手。因此，随着互联网行业的成熟和现有人变得更加根深蒂固，在法国和澳大利亚拥有不同的规则可能并不是那么多缺点 - 或者为此而言，亚利桑那州和北达科他州。
note_en:
Last week, Arizona's House of Representatives  approved legislation to prohibit platform owners like Apple and Google from locking app makers into their own payment systems. The bill passed only narrowly, and it must be approved by the Arizona Senate and Gov. Doug Ducey before it can become law. But regardless of the bill's ultimate fate, the vote is the latest sign of a dramatic shift in public attitudes toward Silicon Valley's most powerful companies.
 For the first two decades of the Internet era, there was a broad consensus that politicians shouldn't tie Silicon Valley companies down with burdensome rules and regulations. Companies like Apple, Amazon, Google, and Uber were widely admired. In 2007, presidential candidates from both parties  made pilgrimages to associate themselves with Google. In 2015, Jeb Bush, Ted Cruz, and other Republican hopefuls  tripped over each other to position themselves as the most Uber-friendly candidate.
  Tech companies' prestige bolstered their political power. Those who proposed regulations to rein in tech companies—or in some cases just wanted to subject them to the same rules as other companies—were often dismissed as out-of-touch reactionaries and enemies of progress. But all that is changing. Products like YouTube, the iPhone, and ride-hailing apps have become banal parts of modern life. The companies behind them are widely considered entrenched incumbents. And so the public and their elected representatives increasingly treat them like other big, powerful companies. Politicians score points by railing against them. Proposals to rein them in sometimes poll well.
 The next few years are going to be particularly challenging for companies like Apple, Google, Facebook, and Uber because they are global companies. They not only have to worry about antitrust lawsuits by the US federal government, they also face heavy scrutiny from US states and from foreign governments around the world. For the tech giants, there's a danger that a policy experiment in one jurisdiction could become a precedent that's copied around the world.
   Back in 2016, the city of Austin, Texas wanted Uber and Lyft to fingerprint drivers—a rule that already applied to regular taxi drivers in the city. The idea was approved by 56 percent of the city's voters. Uber and Lyft responded by  going nuclear—shutting down their Austin operations until the city changed its rules.
 Austin officials argued they were simply requiring Uber and Lyft to play by the same rules as other taxi providers. But many members of the public, especially customers of the services, bought into a different narrative: that Austin officials were out of touch and hostile to modern technology. Having a significant part of the public behind them gave Uber and Lyft a lot of power—power Uber and Lyft ultimately used to get the state legislature to  override Austin's rules.
 This wasn't a one-off victory. It's a playbook the companies used over and over again in their early years. Uber and Lyft would frequently swoop into a new metropolitan area without worrying too much about local taxi regulations. Spending millions in venture capital, they'd try to build up a customer base as quickly as possible. City officials feared a backlash from these customers and they didn't want to be seen as standing in the way of progress. So they almost always relented.
      This strategy has become less effective in recent years. Partly that's because activists have generated more negative press for these firms. Once the gusher of venture-funded subsidies dried up, driving for Uber or Lyft proved to be a low-paying and precarious gig. Critics have also highlighted the problem of sexual assaults occurring in Uber and Lyft vehicles—although it's not clear if Uber and Lyft have a bigger problem here than traditional taxis.
 But perhaps the most important shift is that Uber and Lyft are no longer seen as underdogs. In the early 2010s, these companies' survival still seemed like an open question. Customers worried that incumbent taxi companies would use antiquated regulations to smother them in the cradle. So when Uber and Lyft argued that a particular regulation could force them to leave a city or state, customers believed them and some lobbied their elected officials to back off.
  Today this just isn't a serious concern. Uber and Lyft are multibillion dollar companies operating in dozens of cities. Many jurisdictions have passed legislation explicitly allowing this type of service to operate. As a result, the companies are finding it more and more difficult to beat back efforts to regulate their business practices. In 2019, California  passed legislation requiring Uber and Lyft to treat their drivers as employees. As they've done in the past, Uber and Lyft threatened to shut down their services if voters didn't overturn the law. Voters  ultimately approved a ballot measure sponsored by Uber and Lyft. But Uber and Lyft was forced to make some tactical concessions; the voter-approved measures offered gig economy drivers some added protections.
 And things are going even worse for Uber outside the United States. Last month the Supreme Court of the United Kingdom  ruled that Uber can't treat its drivers like independent contractors. Courts in  France and  Spain have reached similar conclusions—though Uber seems to still be  resisting a switch to an employee model in France.
 Last year,  Massachusetts filed a lawsuit against Uber and Lyft arguing that their drivers are employees under state law. A  class-action lawsuit in Ontario makes a similar claim.
 In each of these jurisdictions, a key Uber argument is that enforcing traditional labor law will break Uber's on-demand business model. That argument is hard to evaluate, since Uber has been mostly been successful in defeating these efforts. But Uber will very likely need to change how it does business in the UK, France, Spain, and probably other jurisdictions in the coming years.
 If Uber manages to come up with a viable model that treats drivers as employees, it will face pressure to adopt the same model in other cities. Though of course the opposite is also possible: maybe a switch to an employee-based model will make Uber's service dramatically worse in places like France and Spain, strengthening Uber's case in future battles.
   Some news organizations have long blamed Google and Facebook for draining away ad revenue that previously went to newspapers and other news media. In 2014, the government of Spain  passed legislation requiring Google to pay to link to news articles.
 Google responded to this legislation in much the same way Uber and Lyft responded to Austin's legislation: by shutting down the Spanish version of Google News. Traffic to Spanish news sites plunged, hurting their advertising revenue. The Spanish government was ultimately forced to capitulate. Like Uber and Lyft in Austin, Google portrayed Spanish officials as out of touch and ignorant of how the Internet worked.
     Google's scorched-earth response to the Spanish law gave the company a few years' reprieve from these "link tax" proposals. But in 2019, they returned with a vengeance. The European Parliament  passed a copyright overhaul that gave news organizations a new right to charge people who reproduced "snippets" of news stories. Officials in France, one of the first countries to implement the new rules,  warned Google that it wouldn't be allowed to simply delist news sites to avoid paying. So Google  started paying. When Australia began debating its own "link tax" proposal targeting both Google and Facebook, the companies threatened to pull their products from the Australian market. In February, Facebook  banned Australian users from posting news stories from Facebook, much as Google had done in Spain seven years earlier. But this time the outcome was rather different. Australian officials  vowed not to let Facebook intimidate them. Facebook did wring some concessions out of the Australian government, but ultimately Australia  passed legislation that effectively requires Google and Facebook to pay to link to news stories.
 Of course, leaders in other countries have been closely monitoring the Australian and French showdowns. Now that those countries shown how to stare down Google and Facebook, we can expect other countries to follow their example. The Canadian government is reportedly  working on legislation similar to Australia's law. And we can expect a lot of EU member states to follow the French model as they implement the EU Copyright Directive.
   The fight in Arizona over app store policies has some similarities to the news and ride-hailing battles, as well as some differences. One important difference is that Apple and Google can't credibly threaten to pull their smartphone platforms out of the Arizona market.
 On the other hand, this is another case where tech companies are battling similar legislative proposals on many different fronts. A few weeks ago, for example, Apple  defeated North Dakota legislation requiring the company to allow iOS apps to use third-party payment mechanisms. Epic Games, Apple's main protagonist in the payment fight, has also made formal complaints to competition regulators in the  European Union, the  United Kingdom, and  Australia.
 It's impossible to predict how any of these specific fights will play out, but it seems likely that Epic will prevail in at least one of these venues. That could force Apple to open up its app store to third-party payments somewhere in the world. And once they've done that, it will be easier for officials in other countries to press Apple to make the same options available for their own consumers and developers.
  Meanwhile, big tech companies are facing broader investigations—and in some cases lawsuits—from a number of governments. The US government along with 52 states and territories  sued Google late last year over anticompetitive behavior in the search and ad markets. Another lawsuit by the Federal Trade Commission and 47 states seeks to  break up Facebook. Several tech giants are  under investigation by European competition authorities. Australian officials have said they see the news bargaining rule as  just the first step in regulating the tech giants. In short, it seems that Silicon Valley companies will increasingly be forced to knuckle under to the policy preferences of governments around the world. Critics of concentrated tech company power may see this as a positive development. But it could also have a big downside: making it more difficult for companies to operate across national borders.
 One of the great things about the Internet is how easy it is for a software company in one nation to expand rapidly and offer its product to customers around the world. But if countries, states, and other jurisdictions adopt a growing thicket of rules governing how online services have to work, it will be harder to offer a single product in multiple jurisdictions. Big companies like Apple and Google will figure it out. Smaller companies might struggle.
 On the other hand, the major technology incumbents haven't faced a lot of serious new competitors in the last decade. So as the Internet industry matures and incumbents become ever more entrenched, there may not be that much downside to having different rules in France and Australia—or for that matter, Arizona and North Dakota.
title: 为什么大型科技正在面临来自澳大利亚的监管威胁到亚利桑那州
title_en: Why Big Tech is facing regulatory threats from Australia to Arizona

DiMare cofounded Bedrock Ocean Exploration last year, with Charles Chiau. The latter brought a depth of robotics expertise to the space, while DiMare has experience with the oceans. His previous company, Nautilus Labs, which specialized in ocean fleet logistical planning, raised  an $11 million Series A back 2019.
 After leaving the startup, DiMare says he met up with Chiau at a San Francisco diner, where the pair discussed the challenges and opportunities in mapping the ocean floor. Today Bedrock is announcing that it has raised an $8 million seed round led by Eniac Ventures, Primary Venture Partners, Quiet Capital and R7.
  The company notes that more than 80% of the ocean remains unmapped. And those parts are often at fairly low resolution. As the CEO puts it in a press release tied this morning’s news, “A far greater percentage of the surfaces of the Moon and Mars have been mapped and studied than our own ocean floor has.”
  The funding will go toward developing partnerships and building out the company’s robotics and cloud platforms. The team, which currently includes some participants from the recent Shell-sponsored XPrize ocean floor competition, should be expanding, as well.
 Among the chief uses for the technology is laying undersea cable. “As of now, it’s done in basically a one-off basis,” says DiMare. “I know that if I need to lay a cable between the United States and China, I’m going to guesstimate the most efficient route and do a survey over that area and hope that it generally returns information that I need to lay a cable. But if I find something, I need to reroute it.”
 Off-shore wind farms are a major potential growth category for the company as well. “Right now we’re not working with any oil companies,” says DiMare. “We didn’t know if we were going to have to go down that route. Thankfully, offshore wind has just absolutely exploded. There’s just so much work to be done in the offshore wind space that we can literally just focus on that.”

Facebook App Monetization Director Yoav Arnstein wrote in  a blog post that creators will now be able to include in-stream ads in videos that are as short as one minute — previously, the minimum was three minutes. Those ads will usually play after 30 seconds of a shorter video.
 “Looking ahead, we’re exploring in-stream ad formats that increase engagement through rewards or product interaction — intending to help content creator payouts grow while providing a good viewing experience for people and a way for advertisers to reach relevant audiences,” Arnstein wrote, adding that the company is “especially focused on short-form video monetization” and will be testing a way to include ads that look like stickers to Facebook Stories.
  Facebook  splits the revenue from these ads with the video creators, and it says it’s also updating the program criteria. To participate, Facebook Pages must  nowhave 600,000 minutes of viewing time across all videos (previously only videos of three minutes or longer had counted) for the last 60 days and five or more active or Live videos.
 On the Live side, Arnstein wrote that Facebook is moving its in-stream advertising program out of invite-only mode, allowing creators with 60,000 minutes of Live viewing in the last 60 days to participate. And it will be investing $7 million to encourage the adoption of Stars (a virtual currency that fans can use to support creators) by offering free Stars.
 Non-advertising products are also continuing their international rollout. Arnstein wrote that paid online events ( launched last summer) are available in 20 countries, with plans to expand to 24 more (including Argentina, Hong Kong and Ireland) in the coming weeks, while  fan subscriptions are available in more than 25 countries and will be introduced in another 10 (Austria, Belgium, Denmark, Finland, Ireland, New Zealand, Norway, Sweden, Switzerland and Turkey).

 随着欧洲联盟在考虑如何考虑如何更广泛地改善GIG工人的条件，所以西班牙在其他欧盟国家的计划中担任员工的塑造，以造成更广泛的区域，因此西班牙担任立法的计划政策。
 西班牙的更广泛的改革旨在支持数字交易商业的增长也有一个强大的社会包容成分，政府说，没有人应该落后于现代化。 
劳工改革协议近年来西班牙的一些法律挑战在送货车手的分类中近年来。法院的成果不同，但去年西班牙最高法院裁定了与同事的就业分类有关的案件，裁定了与快递员的雇用分类有关的案件，因为它还拒绝提出提议欧洲的大教堂。
 市场上的交付平台已经提出了计划改革的影响可能导致成千上万的快递者失去了收入来源。
  还有一些指责的是，平台被视为一个政治上的目标与其他更具成熟的产业依赖于“自雇人士”（自雇）工人提供的劳动力。
 但是，交付初创公司对贸易署的法律要求的法律要求对其商业模式的法律要求较少 - 或者，事实上，他们（正在进行）追求盈利。
 回应西班牙劳动改革协议的新闻，Mangrove Capital Partners的首席执行官Mark Tluszcz  - 谁是Gig平台商业模式的很长一段时间批评 - 告诉TechCrunch：“我们长时间拍摄了演出平台必须经历的观点各国法律驱动的重大结构变化。除非GIG工人被认为是员工，否则我们会冒着创造没有足够权利或社会覆盖的工人的子类。大流行清楚地说明了确保所有工人都有保护的必要性，并且演出平台越来越困难地争论。“
  在今天宣布的改革协议的一个有趣的额外组成部分中，政府表示，收入的立法将要求工人的法律代表通知为用于管理它们的任何算法或AI系统的标准，这可能会影响其工作条件。
 其陈述规定，这包括与访问就业和监视性能或配置文件的任何评级系统相关的算法系统。 
这个组件看起来像欧洲最近的一些法律挑战都采用了灵感，这些挑战侧重于乘坐乘坐平台的算法管理，并驱动程序对数据的访问权限保持在它们上。
 詹姆斯·特勒勒是一名前优步司机，他在英国成功地挑战了公司的就业分类 - 最高法院最近认为挑战的司机是工人 - 也参与了最近的算法和数据访问挑战，已经成立了一个非营利性，目的是为集体谈判的目的为司机建立数据信托。
  西班牙的工会似乎通过推动访问用于管理快递作为解决平台和工人之间的功耗不对称的工具的算法规则来拍摄类似的粘性。
 优步发言人向我们发出本声明以回应西班牙政府的公告：
 “在过去的几周里，全国各地的成千上万的快递员聚集在一起，坚持这一拟议的规定，这些规定将剥夺他们最重要的独立性。在优步，我们完全致力于提高工作标准，并在保持灵活性和控制的同时让独立的工人更多的利益。我们希望与西班牙跨国公司合作，改善独立工作，而不是消除它。“
  “该提案违背了价值灵活的工作的骑手的利益，从送货服务和价值按需交付的送货服务和客户中受益。成千上万的骑手，街头抗议他们渴望自雇人士的愿望已经忽略了他们的声音。
 “交付平台提出了建设性的建议，使骑手能够灵活地与额外的安全性工作，并警告强制重新分类将导致骑手的工作减少，将伤害餐厅行业，并将限制平台可以运作的地区。不幸的是，这些消息也被忽视了。 
“没有什么已经完成，我们将继续争辩说，政府应该为骑手提供灵活性和安全性，这就是他们想要的。 我们将继续与西班牙政府聘用以寻求替代方向。 我们敦促政府倾听骑手，并迫切再次思考。“  最近几周，优步一直在欧盟进程的平台工人放松管制，以提高欧盟改善演出工作的磋商进程 - 看到泛欧框架的可能性作为将区域就业规则更好地与现代工作模式更好的网格的机会 。 然而，此举导致批评它正在努力降低欧盟标准。
note_en:
Once such a law is passed, the development could have major ramifications for platforms operating in the market — which include the likes of Deliveroo, Glovo and UberEats, to name a few.
 “The Ministry of Labor and Social Economy, the trade union organizations CCOO and UGT and the business organizations CEOE and CEPYME have reached an agreement to establish the employment status of workers dedicated to the delivery or distribution of any consumer product or merchandise through platforms digital,” the ministry said in a  statement (which we’ve translated from Spanish)
  “The agreement recognizes the presumption of employment of workers who provide services through digital delivery platforms, in line with the Supreme Court ruling,” it added.
 “The presumption of employment is recognized for workers who provide paid distribution services through companies that manage this work through algorithmic management of the service or working conditions, through a digital platform.”
   Agreement on the reform means the government can now start to move forward with the legislative process after many months negotiating on exactly how to change labor laws.
 The timing looks especially interesting as the European Union is also considering how to improve conditions for gig workers more broadly — so Spain’s plan to legislate, ahead of other EU countries, to recognize a subset of gig workers as employees could be influential in shaping wider regional policy.
 Wider reforms in Spain  aimed at supporting growth of digital business also come with a strong social inclusion component, with the government saying no one should be left behind in a drive to modernize.
  The labor reform agreement follows a number of legal challenges in Spain in recent years over the classification of delivery riders. There have been varying outcomes in the courts but  last year Spain’s Supreme Court ruled against homegrown delivery platform, Glovo, in a case relating to the employment classification of a courier — putting a stamp of finality on the matter as it also declined to refer questions to Europe’s top court.
 Delivery platforms in the market have suggested the impact of the planned reform could result in thousands of couriers losing their source of income.
  There have also been accusations that platforms are being unfairly singled out as a politically easier target vs other more established industries which also rely on labor provided by ‘ autónomos‘ (self-employed) workers.
 However delivery startups have been less vocal about what a legal requirement to put couriers on the payroll would mean for their business model — or, indeed, their (ongoing) quest for profitability.
 Responding to news of Spain’s labor reform agreement, Mark Tluszcz, CEO at Mangrove Capital Partners — who has been a long time critic of the gig platform business model — told TechCrunch: “We have long taken the view that gig platforms would have to go through significant structural changes driven by individual countries’ laws. Unless gig workers are recognized as employees, we risk creating a subclass of workers that don’t have adequate rights or social coverage. The pandemic has clearly illustrated the need to ensure all workers have protections and it is increasingly difficult for gig platforms to argue otherwise.”
  In an interesting additional component to the reform agreement announced today, the government said the incoming legislation will require that workers’ legal representatives are informed of the criteria powering any algorithms or AI systems that are used to manage them and which may affect their working conditions.
 Its statement specifies that this includes algorithmic systems that are related to access to employment and for any rating systems that monitor performance or profile workers.
 This component looks like it’s taking inspiration from a number of recent legal challenges in Europe which have focused on ride-hailing platforms’ algorithmic management and drives’ access to data the platforms hold on them.
 James Farrer, a former Uber driver who successfully challenged the company’s employment classification in the UK — where the  Supreme Court recently held that the challenging drivers are workers — and who is also involved in the more recent algorithm and data access challenges, has set up a not-for-profit with the aim of establishing a data trust for drivers for the purpose of collective bargaining.
  Spain’s unions appear to be taking a similar tack by pushing for access to the algorithmic rules that are used to manage couriers as a tool to tackle the power asymmetry between platforms and workers.
 A spokesperson for Uber sent us this statement in response to the Spanish government’s announcement:
 “Over the past few weeks, thousands of couriers across the country have come together to stand against this proposed regulation that would deprive them from the independence they value most. At Uber, we are fully committed to raising the standard of work and giving independent workers more benefits while preserving flexibility and control. We want to work with all relevant parties across Spain to improve independent work, instead of eliminating it.”
  “This proposal goes against the interests of riders who value flexible work, restaurants who benefit from delivery services and customers who value on-demand delivery. Thousands of riders who took to the streets to protest their desire to remain self-employed have had their voices ignored.
 “Delivery platforms have put forward constructive proposals to enable riders to work flexibly with additional security and have warned that forced reclassification will lead to less work for riders, will hurt the restaurant sector and will restrict the areas where platforms can operate. Unfortunately these messages have also been overlooked.
 “Nothing has been finalised and we will continue to argue that the Government should provide riders with flexibility and security, which is what they want. We will continue to engage with the Spanish Government to seek alternative ways forward. We urge the Government to listen to riders and urgently think again.”
  In recent weeks, Uber has been lobbying for deregulation for platform workers in Europe ahead of the EU’s consultation process on improving gig work — seeing the possibility of a pan-EU framework as an opportunity to reshape regional employment rules to better mesh with modern working patterns. However the move has led to  criticism that it’s trying to lower EU standards.
title: 西班牙同意劳工改革，将承认交付平台车手作为员工
title_en: Spain agrees on labor reform that will recognize delivery platform riders as employees
